did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses deep learning algorithms, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and ResNet-50, to detect and classify melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer. It also employs XGBoost for CSV data classification and utilizes pre-processing techniques like SMOTE and image augmentation to train the models on a dataset from Kaggle/ISIC-Archive, achieving reported accuracies of 95% for the CNN model and 99% for ResNet-50 on training data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a deep learning application for melanoma detection using CNN and ResNet-50, implementation of data pre-processing (SMOTE, augmentation), training on a Kaggle/ISIC dataset, and reporting of new accuracy results (95% for CNN, 99% for ResNet-50 on training data). Although published in a conference proceedings (ASIANCON), it contains original methodology and results rather than just summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection of Melanoma and Non-Melanoma type of Skin Cancer using CNN and RESNET
journal: 2021 Asian Conference on Innovation in Technology (ASIANCON)
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning application to detect and classify melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and ResNet-50. The models were trained on a dataset from the ISIC-Archive and Kaggle, which contained 33,126 images, utilizing pre-processing techniques like SMOTE and image augmentation to balance the data. The basic CNN model achieved an accuracy of 95% on the training set, while the ResNet-50 model achieved 99% accuracy on training images but failed to generalize well to validation data. Additionally, an XGBoost classifier was applied to CSV metadata, achieving 82% accuracy. The authors concluded that emphasizing pre-processing methods is crucial for improving detection accuracy and suggested that future work should focus on hybrid models and larger datasets to enhance ResNet-50's performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on detecting melanoma as the malignant class. The dataset (SIIM-ISIC) contained 584 malignant images (initially) which were oversampled to 6000 using SMOTE. The study classifies images as 'benign or malignant' with malignant corresponding to melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer / Benign Skin Lesions
lesion_types_studied.details: The paper aims to detect 'non-melanoma type of skin cancer' and benign conditions. The dataset contained 32,542 benign images. The classification task is binary: benign vs. malignant (melanoma). While 'non-melanoma' is mentioned in the title and abstract, the specific histological subtypes (e.g., BCC, SCC) are not explicitly detailed as separate experimental classes in the methodology, but rather grouped under the 'benign' or 'non-melanoma' category in the context of the binary classification task.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'classify if a given image is benign or malignant' using CNN and ResNet-50 models. The dataset labels are 'benign' and 'malignant'. Additionally, an XGBoost classifier was used on CSV metadata to achieve 82% accuracy for the same binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Dataset (Kaggle/ISIC-Archive)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the Kaggle SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification competition and the ISIC Archive, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions along with associated CSV metadata. The original dataset contained 33,126 images.

use.training: Used to train the CNN and ResNet-50 models. The malignant class was oversampled from 584 to 6000 using SMOTE, resulting in a balanced training set of 12,000 images (6000 benign, 6000 malignant).
use.validation: Used for validation of the ResNet-50 model, though specific counts or split details were not reported; the model failed to generalize well to this data.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out test set with counts; accuracy metrics were primarily reported on training data (95% for CNN, 99% for ResNet-50).
use.other: CSV metadata from this dataset was used to train an XGBoost classifier, achieving 82% accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are reported as accuracy percentages (e.g., 95% for CNN, 99% for ResNet-50 on training data) without statistical significance analysis.

CNN (Basic CNN with preprocessing).notes: Trained on 12,000 images (6,000 benign, 6,000 malignant after SMOTE oversampling of malignant class from 584 to 6,000). Achieved 95% accuracy on training data.

ResNet-50.notes: Achieved 99% accuracy on training images. However, the model did not generalize well to validation data. The paper notes the dataset was small for this model and suggests future work with larger datasets could increase accuracy to 97%.

XGBoost.notes: Applied to CSV metadata from the dataset.

SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Datase.notes: The paper compares the proposed CNN model (95% accuracy) against other algorithms from literature (GAN, SSAE, Inception v3) which gave accuracies of less than 95%. The ResNet-50 model failed to generalize to validation data despite high training accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Basic CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) and ResNet-50
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (ResNet-50 is a standard architecture, but specific pretraining weights or source are not explicitly stated in the context; the paper focuses on implementation and training from scratch or fine-tuning without specifying the source).

basic_cnn.description: A basic CNN model with 3 convolutional layers.
basic_cnn.feature_extraction: Laplacian and Canny methods used for feature extraction prior to classification.

resnet_50.notes: Noted to have less complexity with respect to the number of layers compared to other models, encourages feature reuse, and substantially reduces the number of parameters.

training_details.dataset_size: 12,000 images (6,000 benign, 6,000 malignant after SMOTE oversampling of the malignant class from 584 to 6,000).
preprocessing: Color Gradient Generation (Sobel operators: Sobel x, Sobel y, Sobel OR)
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction (Otsu’s method, threshold binary)

classification_or_output_head.additional_model: XGBoost classifier used for CSV metadata classification.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported (Paper notes ResNet reduces parameters compared to other algorithms but does not give a specific count).

preprocessing_techniques: Data Augmentation (Warp perspective/rotation to balance dataset and extract features)
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-Sampling Technique) to oversample the malignant class from 584 to 6000 images
Color Gradient Generation using Sobel operators (Sobel x, Sobel y, and modified Sobel OR) to highlight depth and remove unwanted pigments/hair
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction using Otsu’s method, threshold binary, image erosion, dilation, and opening to separate normal skin from affected skin
Image enhancement and segmentation (Image Scaling) to bring affected parts into focus
Feature Extraction using Laplacian and Canny methods
General image processing techniques mentioned for accuracy improvement: Grey Image, Sharpening Image, Median Filter, Smooth Filter, Binary Mask, Histogram, YCbCr

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated to extract more features and balance the dataset. Used as a geometric transform during pre-processing.

Used to oversample the malignant class from 584 to 6000 images by selecting similar instances and perturbing attributes randomly within the difference to neighboring instances. This is a sampling method for class balancing.
Sobel x, Sobel y, and modified Sobel OR operators were used to highlight depth and remove unwanted pigments and hair. This serves as a color/intensity transform for feature enhancement.
Otsu’s method, threshold binary, image erosion, dilation, and opening were used to separate normal skin from affected skin. This acts as a filtering/thresholding technique.

Image scaling was used to bring affected parts into focus and segment the area. General preprocessing techniques mentioned include Grey Image, Sharpening Image, Median Filter, Smooth Filter, Binary Mask, Histogram, and YCbCr.
Laplacian and Canny methods were used for feature extraction to identify important information about the image.

conclusions: The implemented basic CNN model, combined with various preprocessing algorithms (including SMOTE and image augmentation), achieved an accuracy of 95% on the training dataset, outperforming existing methods like GAN, SSAE, and Inception v3 which yielded accuracies of less than 95%.
The ResNet-50 model achieved 99% accuracy on training images but failed to generalize well to validation data, likely due to the small size of the dataset used for training.
An XGBoost classifier applied to CSV metadata achieved an accuracy of 82% for the classification task.
The study concludes that greater emphasis should be placed on preprocessing methods to improve the accuracy of skin cancer detection.

Future work should focus on using larger datasets to improve ResNet-50 performance (potentially increasing accuracy to 97%) and exploring hybrid models consisting of two or more techniques to achieve high accuracy with low complexity.
important_remarks: The primary accuracy metrics (95% for CNN, 99% for ResNet-50) are reported on the training dataset, not a held-out test set, which significantly overestimates generalization performance.
The ResNet-50 model failed to generalize to validation data despite 99% training accuracy, indicating severe overfitting likely due to the small effective dataset size for deep architectures.

The malignant class was artificially balanced from 584 to 6,000 samples using SMOTE oversampling, which may introduce synthetic bias and does not reflect the true prevalence of melanoma in clinical populations.
The study lacks external validation; the model was trained and evaluated on a single Kaggle/ISIC dataset, limiting the assessment of robustness across different imaging devices or populations.
No statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) were reported to validate the claimed superiority over baseline methods.

The binary classification task (Benign vs. Malignant) groups all non-melanoma skin cancers together, preventing the detection of specific non-melanoma subtypes (e.g., BCC, SCC) which have different clinical implications.
The paper compares its CNN results against literature values in tables rather than re-implementing and testing those baselines on the same data, making direct performance comparisons unreliable.

Deployment concerns exist as the application was deployed on Heroku and an Android app, but no information is provided regarding latency, computational requirements, or integration with clinical workflows.
